chosen_title: Scientists Found Ancient Human DNA on Cave Walls for the First Time and It Could Rewrite the Story of Prehistoric Art
label: SCIENCE
headline: Their DNA Was in the Walls
image_prompt: A prehistoric ochre handprint stencil pressed onto rough limestone deep inside a cave, the surrounding wall covered in faint animal figures and abstract marks made tens of thousands of years ago. The cave chamber recedes into absolute darkness behind it, giving a sense of vast subterranean depth and age. In the foreground, a gloved researcher's hand hovers a centimeter above the ancient surface, holding a slender sampling swab — about to collect something invisible to the naked eye. Fine dust and pigment particles drift through the narrow beam of a bright accent glow from a headlamp, suggesting the microscopic biological traces suspended in rock and air. Keep the lower third of the frame in deep shadow.
tweets: Scientists just found ancient human DNA on cave walls — not from bones, not from soil. From the walls where prehistoric people stood and made their art. We can now find out exactly who painted them. 🧵

Until now, ancient human DNA came almost entirely from bones and teeth. But researchers have recovered genetic material directly from rock art surfaces — the first time it has ever been successfully done.

How does DNA end up on a cave wall? Every time a prehistoric artist worked, they left biology behind:
• Skin cells shed during touch
• Saliva and breath from leaning in close
• Sweat pressed into stone with pigment
A biological signature alongside every brushstroke.

This matters because the identity of prehistoric artists has always been a black box. Bones nearby tell us about the dead. DNA on the walls tells us about the living — the specific individuals who stood there and made something.

What this now unlocks:
• Who created specific panels — sex, age, genetic ancestry
• Whether multiple people collaborated on a single painting
• How artists at sites thousands of miles apart may have been genetically connected

The study also raises a harder question: how much ancient DNA is already coating the walls of undiscovered caves — slowly degrading while we debate whether to enter them?
40,000 years after someone pressed their hand to stone, we can now read what they left behind.
